Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed again. Let me inspect what happened after clicking the purple square's magenta center. I need to see where the white diamond moved to and how the path reconfigured.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking purple square center
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Find the white diamond (12 pixels) and key objects
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] >= 10:
        print(f"White diamond: id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}, children={n['children']}")
        if n['children']:
            for cid in n['children']:
                cn = next(x for x in nodes if x['id'] == cid)
                print(f"  Child: color={cn['color']}, boundary={cn['boundary']}")
    elif n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] >= 10:
        print(f"Dark gray diamond: id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}, children={n['children']}")
        if n['children']:
            for cid in n['children']:
                cn = next(x for x in nodes if x['id'] == cid)
                print(f"  Child: color={cn['color']}, boundary={cn['boundary']}")
    elif n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] >= 15:
        print(f"Purple square: id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}, children={n['children']}")
        if n['children']:
            for cid in n['children']:
                cn = next(x for x in nodes if x['id'] == cid)
                print(f"  Child: color={cn['color']}, boundary={cn['boundary']}")

# Find gray path segments
print("\nGray path segments:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'w' and n['pixels'] <= 5:
        print(f"  w pixels: {n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Find purple ring
print("\nPurple ring pixels:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        b = n['boundary'][0]
        if 18 <= b[0] <= 24 and 36 <= b[1] <= 42:
            print(f"  at {b}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking on an object center moves the white diamond to that position and reconfigures the path"}, "object_taxonomy": {"purple_ring": "hollow diamond ring at ~21,39", "white_diamond": "12-pixel white diamond that moves when clicked", "gray_path": "reconfigurable diagonal path connecting objects", "purple_square": "20-pixel purple square with magenta center", "dark_gray_diamond": "12-pixel dark gray diamond with purple center"}, "hud_map": {}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "After 2 clicks: white diamond moved from ring to purple square position. Path reconfigured. Need to find where objects are now.", "goal_model": "Move objects along path in sequence", "recent_findings": ["White diamond follows clicks", "Path reconfigures each time"], "open_questions": ["Where is white diamond now?", "What's the final target?"], "current_plan": "Inspect current positions, then click next object", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the pattern:
1. White diamond (12 pixels) is now at the purple square's old position, with purple center at [40,33]
2. Purple square (20 pixels) moved - its magenta center is now at [49,30]
3. Dark gray diamond (12 pixels) still at [59,27] with purple center
4. Gray path connects from white diamond area down to purple square and then to dark gray diamond
The pattern seems to be: clicking on an object's center moves the white diamond there, and the clicked object moves along the path toward the next object. The objects are moving in a chain along the path.
So the sequence is:
- Ring position (top) → Purple square → Dark gray diamond (bottom)
After clicking the ring center, white diamond went there and purple square moved toward dark gray diamond.
After clicking purple square center, white diamond went there and purple square moved further toward dark gray diamond.
Now I should click the purple square's new magenta center at [49,30] to continue the chain. Eventually the objects should all reach the dark gray diamond or some final position.
Let me click on the magenta center of the purple square at [49,30].

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Purple square's magenta center moved to [49,30]
# Click it to continue the chain
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 49, 'col': 30}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

Purple square magenta center now at [49,30].", "goal_model": "Move all objects to the dark gray diamond position", "recent_findings": ["Chain movement pattern confirmed", "Objects move toward dark gray diamond"], "open_questions": ["How many clicks to complete?", "What triggers level completion?"], "current_plan": "Keep clicking purple square center as it moves along path", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
